title: Jean Eustache: the outsider who reshaped French cinema
gist: Jean Eustache, the working-class outsider of the Nouvelle Vague, used personal ethnography (rural documentaries and autofiction like The Mother and the Whore) to register a sardonic scepticism toward May '68 ideology, exposing a deeply conservative French temperament beneath the country's revolutionary reputation.

insights.claim: Eustache's films register sardonic scepticism that May '68 ideologues could change the nature of relationships between men and women, depicting post-'68 'open' relationships as just as restrictive as old courtship rules.
insights.speaker: Engelsberg Ideas
insights.so_what: A contrarian read on the 1968 cultural revolution as failing to deliver real liberation.
insights.quote: post-'68 'open' relationships were just as restrictive and oppressive as the old rules of courtship

insights.claim: Eustache suggests there is no exit from rules and untrammelled freedom proves an illusion, a frame at odds with the era's utopian self-image.
insights.speaker: Engelsberg Ideas
insights.so_what: A durable critique of the limits of liberation movements.
insights.quote: There is, in fact, no exit from rules

insights.claim: Eustache's work reveals a French temperament very different from the common perception of France as emotionally and politically eruptive; outside Paris, France is at heart a profoundly conservative country.
insights.speaker: Engelsberg Ideas
insights.so_what: Challenges the cliché of revolutionary France, useful context for reading French political stability.
insights.quote: Outside of Paris, France is at heart a profoundly conservative country
insights.locator: is a picture of the French temperament

insights.claim: Major French upheavals (the Revolution, May '68, the gilets jaunes) have each been followed by a robust return to social stability.
insights.speaker: Engelsberg Ideas
insights.so_what: A pattern suggesting French unrest tends to revert to stability rather than structural change.
insights.quote: the reaction to (and aftermath of) all these events has been a robust return to social stability

insights.claim: Eustache practised filmmaking as personal ethnography, recording reality verbatim rather than as auteur fiction, an approach later inherited by directors from Despléchin to Mia Hansen-Løve.
insights.speaker: Engelsberg Ideas
insights.so_what: Names a transmissible artistic method that propagated through later French cinema.
insights.quote: the ethnologist of his own reality

insights.claim: Eustache held that the most revolutionary thing to do in 1970s France was to cleave to tradition and take 'big steps backwards' to the Lumiere brothers' approach.
insights.speaker: Engelsberg Ideas
insights.so_what: Inverts the avant-garde assumption that radicalism requires novelty.
insights.locator: Eustache believed that the best way to be revolutionary
